methodology_long: Rankings are generated by a shadow roto model. Each week, all twelve teams are scored across the fourteen active categories (R, HR, RBI, SB, AVG, OBP, SLG, W, K, ERA, WHIP, K/BB, QS, SV+H) based on an estimate of each team's true strength — not by their actual head-to-head matchup result, which depends on schedule luck. Each team receives 12 points for first in a category down to 1 point for last; the Power Score is the sum (max 168, min 14), and that's the rank. True strength is estimated with a Bayesian prior (the preseason projection) blended with each completed week's real performance using exponential decay, so recent weeks count more than older ones. Rate categories (ERA, AVG, etc.) are computed from weighted components (e.g., total earned runs over total innings pitched), not from averaging the week-level rates.

rankings.blurb: Solidified at #1 with a power score of 135 — the highest single-team score the model has produced all season. Beat Y&Y 9-5-2 in Week 9 with another balanced line: 43 R, 6 HR, 33 RBI, 8 SB, .244/.339/.376, plus 6 W, 79 K, 2.24 ERA, 1.02 WHIP, 7 QS, 6 SV+H over 80.1 IP. Leads the league in K and ranks top-3 in nine of fourteen cats. The soft cells remain SV+H (8th) and QS (6th); neither is fixable without a roster move, but the rest of the team is so deep that it doesn't matter.

Tied Camp 6-6-4 in Week 9 — a flat result that holds them at #2. Solid pitching line (74 IP, 6 W, 71 K, 4.62 ERA, 1.27 WHIP, 4 QS, 8 SV+H) but the bats were thin: 41 R, 11 HR, 25 RBI, 2 SB on a .235/.327/.408 slash. Still leads the league in W and QS, ranks 2nd in WHIP and K/BB, 3rd in SV+H and OBP. The structural profile hasn't changed all season — elite pitching floor with mid-tier offense.

Held at #3 but the underlying roster transformed mid-Week 9. A major trade with Polar Express on May 20 sent Kurtz, Painter, Brown, Flaherty, Civale, Montero, May (plus picks) out and brought back Alonso, Trea Turner, Altuve, Valdez, Suarez, Sheehan, Yamamoto, Eovaldi (plus picks). Despite the disruption, UBC posted its biggest matchup win of the season — 11-3-2 over PO' BOYD. Week 9 line: 40 R, 12 HR, 37 RBI, 10 SB, 5 QS, 8 SV+H, 3.40 ERA, 1.08 WHIP — finally a complete week. The model's preseason prior is now stale for this team; the new roster's full impact won't show in the rankings until W11–W12.

Up one. Beat Spoons 7-6-3 in a tight matchup that came down to a couple of cats. Pitching had a strong week (65 IP, 6 W, 67 K, 2.63 ERA, 1.22 WHIP, 5 QS) and the offense held up (39 R, 8 HR, 27 RBI, .263 AVG, 7 SB). Best actual matchup record in the league at 7-1-1. The model still has them lower than the W-L because the cumulative power profile has no top-3 cats outside of HR, K, and QS. Solid floor but no spike.

Up one despite losing to Caminero 6-7-3. The pitching kept its season-long form (67 IP, 5 W, 54 K, 3.22 ERA, 1.12 WHIP, 5 QS) and now leads the league in ERA, WHIP, AND K/BB — three first-place rate cats. The offensive structural weakness is still real: 40 R / 9 HR / 30 RBI in W9, and cumulative ranks of 11th in HR, RBI, and AVG. SV+H is dead-last (11th).

Drops two after a brutal Week 9 — lost to UBC 11-3-2 with only 24 R, 3 W, 2 SV+H. The offense, which has been the team's calling card, scored less than every team except Polar. Still leads the league in SLG and ranks 3rd in HR and AVG, but the SB (9th), W (10th), and K/BB (12th) profile keeps capping the ceiling. Actual matchup record (2-5-2) is well behind the power score.

Held at #7. Lost to CLAUDE 5-9-2 despite an enormous offensive week (.302 AVG, 81 hits, 40 R, 7 HR, 40 RBI) — the best AVG and R of any team in W9. Now leads the league in AVG. But the pitching was a disaster again: 4.11 ERA, 1.20 WHIP, only 3 QS in 65.2 IP. Cumulative ranks remain bottom-three in ERA, WHIP, and QS. Same story all season.

Lost to ShawSwank 6-7-3. Pitching cooled (60.2 IP, 4 W, 76 K, 3.86 ERA, 1.30 WHIP, 0 SV+H!) — the bullpen hasn't recorded a SV+H in two weeks. Cumulative ranks still strong in W (2nd), QS (3rd), ERA (3rd), but the SV+H freefall is now at 12th (last). Matchup record (1-6-2) reflects the structural issue: great rate cats can't win cats by themselves.

Beat Balls 7-6-3 to push to 6-3 actual record — third-best in the league. The pitching delivered (79.2 IP, 5 W, 69 K, 4.41 ERA, 1.21 WHIP, 5 QS, 9 SV+H). Cumulative ranks 2nd in SV+H and K, 3rd in W. The offensive profile is still last or near-last in HR (12), RBI (12), and SLG (11) — those drags are why the model can't lift the team above #9 despite the matchup record.

rankings.team_name: Camp of the New Orleans Saints
rankings.blurb: Tied Burns 6-6-4 with the best Camp pitching week of the year: 85.1 IP (most by any team in W9), 4 W, 74 K, 2.74 ERA, 1.10 WHIP, 4 QS, 7 SV+H. The Sánchez/Castillo/Gausman rotation finally aligned. Offense was decent too (38 R, 7 HR, 25 RBI, 8 SB). Cumulative profile still ranked 5th in K/BB and SB but bottom-three in SV+H, ERA, and WHIP — the season-long sample hasn't caught up to recent form.

Up one out of last. Demolished Polar 12-1-3 — the largest matchup blowout of the season. Week 9 line was a complete performance: 31 R, 11 HR, 50 RBI (best RBI total of any team this week), 7 SB, .264/.347/.471, 6 W, 71 K, 6 QS, 4.02 ERA. José Ramírez and the lineup finally clicked at the same time as the pitching. Still last or near-last in AVG, SLG, and WHIP cumulatively — but this is the most positive trajectory of any bottom-five team.

Drops to last after getting destroyed by Duran 1-12-3. Lost the entire SP rotation in the May 20 trade with UBC — Valdez, Suarez, Sheehan, Yamamoto, and Eovaldi are all gone, replaced by Kurtz and a pile of mid-tier SPs (Painter, Brown, Flaherty, Civale, Montero, May). The model still uses the preseason prior, which now significantly overstates Polar's pitching. The actual W10+ output will reflect the gutted rotation. Long way back.
